characters.blurb: Athenian of decent but middling house; Themistocles’s hunger for greatness began in that modest shadow.
Invaded Greece with a vast host. Themistocles’s messages and timing at Salamis drew the Persian fleet into the narrows.
Ostracized amid Themistocles’s rise, then recalled against Xerxes; at Salamis the old enemies worked as one.

characters.relation: Spartan admiral of the allied fleet
characters.blurb: Nominal commander at Artemisium and Salamis; Themistocles managed him with argument, threats, and bribes.
Victor of Plataea whose Medism and arrogance later tainted Sparta—and entangled Themistocles in suspicion.
Rose as Themistocles faded; more acceptable to the aristocrats, he inherited Athens’s war against Persia.
His glory at Marathon was the standard Themistocles burned to surpass by making Athens a sea power.

characters.relation: Persian court figure in the exile tale
characters.blurb: Named in the intrigue around Themistocles’s reception in Asia after his flight from Greece.

characters.relation: Trusted servant; false message to Xerxes
characters.blurb: Pedagogue sent to tell Xerxes the Greeks were trapped and ready to flee—so the King would fight in the straits.
Former enemy who received Themistocles as a suppliant at the hearth when Athens and Sparta hunted him.

characters.relation: Host on the road to the King
characters.blurb: Aeolian who hid Themistocles and conveyed him inland toward the Persian court in a closed litter.
Abusive lyric poet who lampooned Themistocles as corrupt and Medizing—a voice of the hate he left behind.
Cean poet who shared Themistocles’s company; Plutarch uses their exchanges to show the general’s wit and vanity.
Named among Themistocles’s children; her marriages mark how his house spread even in exile’s shadow.
Ambitious man Themistocles bought off so he would not hold a generalship in the Persian crisis.
Commander Themistocles coaxed (and paid) to stay and fight when the Greek fleet wavered.
Xerxes’s lieutenant who stayed to finish the land war after Salamis; background to Plataea’s later victory.
Thasian writer Plutarch quotes for anecdotes of Themistocles, Cimon, and Pericles.
Mentioned as the statesman who inherited the naval Athens Themistocles had forced into being.
Appears in a story illustrating Themistocles’s sharp tongue among foreign princes.
Eresian author cited for details of Themistocles’s reception in Persia and death.
Praised Themistocles’s foresight—especially the Piraeus—more coolly than comic poets had.
Among later writers who weighed Themistocles’s cleverness against Aristides’s virtue.
Asian Greek city from which Themistocles drew bread, meat, and wine in the King’s grant—gilded exile.

Athenian of middling birth whose foresight built the Piraeus and the fleet that broke Xerxes at Salamis. Ostracized by the city he saved, he fled to Persia, accepted a satrapy, and died—whether by choice or poison—before he could fight Athens again.
One tradition named a Thracian woman called Abrotonon (or Euterpe of Halicarnassus in another) as Themistocles's mother—his obscure birth part of what drove his ferocious ambition.
Gave Themistocles his most famous endorsement—the praise of his ability to see at a glance what a situation required—which Thucydides and Plutarch repeat and refine.
Chian historian Plutarch cites for a different, larger reckoning of Themistocles's children and marriages—evidence that the general's domestic life was as various as his politics.
Athenian who told Themistocles, before the allied council could dissolve, that the one thing needed was to fight in the strait—the counsel Themistocles brought to Eurybiades as if it were his own.
Led Athenian forces in the destruction of the Persian fleet at Mycale after Salamis; as a political rival, he also once prosecuted Miltiades—a reminder that Athenian gratitude was never lasting.

characters.relation: Xerxes's admiral-brother who fell at Salamis
characters.blurb: Xerxes's bravest brother commanded part of the Persian fleet; his death in the battle of Salamis removed the most formidable of the king's naval commanders.
Cited by Plutarch for details about the children of Themistocles, including the daughter's marriages—one of the minor sources that cluster around the exile years.

characters.relation: Goddess of the temple he built and named
characters.blurb: Themistocles built a shrine to Artemis 'of the Best Counsel' in his deme Melite—naming the goddess after his own strategic gift, a typical piece of Themistoclean self-promotion.

characters.relation: Son who recovered his bones for Athens
characters.blurb: Named as the son who eventually brought Themistocles's bones back from Magnesia to be re-buried secretly in Attica, according to the exiled general's own wish.
Among the children of Themistocles, Cleophantus was remembered as a brilliant horseman—a fact Plutarch preserves from Plato's dialogue on teachable virtue.

characters.relation: Goddess whose serpent deserted Athens before Salamis
characters.blurb: The sacred serpent kept in Athena's temple disappeared before the battle—a portent the priests used to urge Athenians to abandon the city. Plutarch also tells the story of Athena and Neptune disputing for Athens.

characters.relation: God whose oracle directed Themistocles in exile
characters.blurb: The oracle of Dodona commanded Themistocles to go to the man who bore a name like Jupiter's own—leading him to Admetus.

characters.relation: Demi-god compared to the low-born achiever
characters.blurb: Plutarch invokes Hercules—himself of mixed divine and mortal blood—as an analogy for Themistocles, who was born outside full civic standing yet surpassed those of pure birth.

characters.relation: Philosopher said to have taught him
characters.blurb: Some sources place Themistocles among Anaxagoras's hearers, though Plutarch notes this is chronologically improbable—Melissus commanded Samos decades after Salamis.

characters.relation: Philosopher said to have taught him
characters.blurb: Eleatic philosopher named alongside Anaxagoras as a possible teacher of Themistocles—an attribution Plutarch doubts on chronological grounds.
Father of Aristides the Just—named by Plutarch to identify the rival whom Themistocles spent his youth contesting.

locations.relation: Home city; builder of the fleet and the Piraeus
locations.blurb: He diverted the Laurium silver to build triremes and enclosed the Piraeus—making Athens a sea power for generations. Ostracized by the city he saved, he fled and never returned alive.

locations.relation: Pass briefly defended before retreat
locations.blurb: Led Greek forces north to hold the Tempe pass against Xerxes; on further consideration the position was untenable, and the army sailed back—clearing the way for the naval strategy at Artemisium.

locations.relation: Sea-battles that trained the Greek fleet
locations.blurb: Three days of fighting in the Euboean straits: neither decisive, but they gave the Greeks the confidence that numbers and wealth were not everything. Themistocles managed Eurybiades with bribes and argument.

locations.relation: Decisive battle that broke Xerxes
locations.blurb: Themistocles sent Sicinnus to lure the Persian fleet into the narrows, then fought it there. Xerxes watched from his throne as his armada was destroyed. Greece was saved.

locations.relation: First island demanded tribute after Salamis
locations.blurb: After Salamis, Themistocles sailed among the islands demanding money; at Andros he claimed two goddesses—Persuasion and Force—and the Andrians replied they had two equally powerful ones: Poverty and Helplessness.

locations.relation: City of his first exile
locations.blurb: After ostracism he settled at Argos; here the detection of Pausanias's treason gave his enemies at Athens and Sparta the weapon they needed to indict him for treason as well.

locations.relation: Brief refuge; obligations from a past arbitration
locations.blurb: Passed over to Corcyra when officers were sent to take him; the state owed him thanks for an earlier arbitration. But fearing they might not dare protect him against Athens and Sparta, he sailed on.

locations.relation: Admetus's kingdom; sanctuary at the hearth
locations.blurb: Though Admetus had once been his enemy, Themistocles threw himself at the king's hearth as a suppliant—the sacred gesture no host could refuse. Admetus sheltered him until the hunt moved on.

locations.relation: Macedonian port where he boarded ship for Asia
locations.blurb: From Pydna in the bay of Therme he took ship anonymously; a storm nearly blew him into the Athenian besieging fleet at Naxos before the captain steered clear and landed him on the Asiatic coast.

locations.relation: Aeolian city where the hunt closed in
locations.blurb: Arrived in Aeolia at Cyme and learned that watchers lined every stretch of coast; the Persians had posted a reward of two hundred talents. He fled quickly to the smaller city of Aegae nearby.

locations.relation: Small Aeolian city; Nicogenes hid him here
locations.blurb: A small city of the Aeolians where only his host Nicogenes, richest man in the region and known to the Persian court, could safely conceal him. From here Nicogenes devised the litter escape into Asia.

locations.relation: Visited the temples on his Persian progress
locations.blurb: After winning the king's favor, Themistocles came to Sardis and toured the temples at leisure. He saw in the goddess's precinct the water-carrier statue he himself had commissioned for Athens years before.

locations.relation: Persian fief; place of his death and tomb
locations.blurb: The king gave Magnesia for his bread: here Themistocles lived as a Persian grandee, was honored with great dinners and processions, and died—by poison of his own choosing, Plutarch says—when ordered to march against Greece. His splendid tomb stood in the market-place.

locations.relation: His great monument to Athenian sea power
locations.blurb: Themistocles fortified and enlarged the Piraeus, building it into the great naval harbor of Athens; Thucydides praises the Piraeus as his lasting gift to the city more than any single victory.

locations.relation: Refuge for Athenian families before Salamis
locations.blurb: Most Athenians removed their wives and children to Troezen before Salamis; the Troezenians voted stipends for the refugees and provided teachers for the children—a generosity remembered long after.

locations.relation: Ally managed, later persecutor
locations.blurb: Themistocles handled Sparta's admiral Eurybiades with argument and bribes to keep the fleet at Salamis. Years later Sparta joined Athens in prosecuting him for treason—the gratitude of great powers.

locations.relation: Victory that obsessed him as a rival standard
locations.blurb: The trophy of Marathon and Miltiades's glory was the standard Themistocles burned to surpass; when others slept soundly, he lay awake haunted by it—the spur that built the fleet.

locations.relation: Island rival; target of the first fleet-building vote
locations.blurb: The Aeginetans were the most flourishing sea power in Greece before the Persian war; Themistocles's first great political act was persuading Athens to build warships against them rather than distribute the silver.

locations.relation: Persian grant-city for wine
locations.blurb: One of the three cities the Persian king gave Themistocles: Lampsacus for wine, Magnesia for bread, Myus for meat—a gilded satrapy on the Hellespont, far from the Athens that had cast him out.

locations.relation: Persian grant-city for meat
locations.blurb: Myus for meat—the third city of Themistocles's Persian allowance, together with Magnesia and Lampsacus. The three cities gave him all the material comfort of a great lord, in the service of his former enemies.
